patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock contention events. The patch uses an arch-overridable function to ensure correct behavior across all architectures, including those with custom unlock implementations. When the tracepoint is disabled, it has minimal impact on performance, adding only a single NOP instruction to the hot path. However, when enabled, it can increase binary size by up to 0.24% due to the need to preserve the lock pointer in a callee-saved register across the trace call.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called queued_spin_release(), which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It renames existing arch-specific queued_spin_unlock() overrides on x86 (paravirt) and MIPS to queued_spin_release(). The change is preparatory for the next commit, which will add contended release tracepoint instrumentation to queued_spin_unlock(). No functional changes are made.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The tracepoint fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing developers to correlate lock hold times with waiter events by lock address. The patch includes modifications to various slowpath unlock paths for sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, rwsems, and percpu-rwsems. For reader/writer locks, the tracepoint fires for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original percpu_up_read() is simplified and no longer contains the slowpath, which is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint to an inlined function. The patch also adds an export for the new function.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides two main benefits: it allows for hold time estimation by correlating contention_end and contended_release events, and it captures the holder's stack at release time, which can be different from what is captured earlier if the holder does significant work between the waiter's arrival and unlock. The patch also fixes several issues in the previous versions, including spurious events, use-after-free errors, and build failures on certain architectures.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox questioned the usefulness of the contended_release tracepoint, specifically asking for explanations on how it provides value in measuring hold time and capturing the holder's stack at release time.
review_comments.raw_body:
As someone who's not an expert in this area (so please use short words
to explain it to me), why do we want to know how long this holder took
to release the lock from when it became contended?

I understand why we want to know how long any given waiter had to wait
to gain the lock (but we already have tracepoints which show that).

I also don't understand why we want to know the holder's stack at
release time.  The stack at contention-begin time will include
the point at which the lock was acquired which should be correlated
with where the lock was released.

Perhaps examples might help me understand why we want this?

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
Steven Rostedt noted that the tracepoint placement for queued spinlocks and rwlocks should fire before the lock is released, not after, to accurately capture the holder's stack at release time.

The reviewer, Steven Rostedt, pointed out that the contended_release tracepoint would be useful for determining how long a critical section is held when a lock is highly contended by many tasks. He suggested that seeing the release time and location would help identify if the length of the critical section is causing the contention.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 26 Mar 2026 15:55:21 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

Dmitry could give his own rationale for this, but I have my only use case.

This would be useful to find out how long the critical section is. If a
lock is highly contended by many tasks, you could get a high contention
time simply because other tasks are causing the delay for the waiter.
Seeing the release time and location would let you also know how long the
critical section was held, and if the length of the critical section is
causing the contention.

Having a stack trace of the release would differentiate the path that
released the lock, as there can be many places that release them. Although,
I have to admit, I'm not sure there are many different places locks are
released. Especially now that we have guard(), which will make all the
releases in a function at the same location.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Matthew Wilcox's suggestion to come up with generic instrumentation for contended release tracepoint, explaining that the current implementation provides a cause (holder time) and symptom (waiter time), but requires different treatments depending on the lock usage pattern.

The author addressed Matthew Wilcox's concern that the --lock-owner feature does not work for all lock types by explaining that contended_release tracepoint fires in the holder's context, providing the holder's stack at release time, which is necessary to identify the holders and what they were doing.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the simplest way to think about it is the following. Waiter time
is the symptom, while holder time is the cause.

The waiter-side contention_begin/contention_end tells us how long a
waiter waited, but that time can span multiple holders.

If a waiter waited 10 ms, we can not tell whether one holder held the
lock for 10 ms or five holders held it for 2 ms each. These need
different treatments: the first means shrink the critical section, the
second means reduce lock frequency or split the lock. Today we can not
distinguish between these cases from waiter-side data alone.

---

Holder's stack allows us to understand who exactly waiters were waiting
for to release the lock.

The stack at contention_begin time does not always include the holder's
stack. The --lock-owner feature works by reading the owner field from
the lock struct, but it only supports mutex and rwsem. For spinlocks,
queued rwlocks, semaphores, and several others, the waiter has no
visibility into the holder whatsoever.

contended_release fires in the holder's context, so we get the holder's
stack at release time. For spinlocks, this is the only way to get any
holder-side information.

Original motivation was zone lock contention (a spinlock) in Meta
production workloads. We could see waiters were blocked, but had no way
to identify the holders or what they were doing.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference any symbols from linux/sched.h.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [patch v2 09/14] genirq: Expose irq_find_desc_at_or_after() in core code
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch v2 of genirq: Expose irq_find_desc_at_or_after() in core code is being reviewed for a potential RCU-related bug.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Raised concern about a potential double-put bug in irq_find_at_or_after() due to RCU and lockdep interactions.

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Confirmed the potential issue and added a lockdep assert to irq_find_at_or_after() after re-checking with lockdep enabled.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Mar 26 2026 at 19:13, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
>> -static unsigned int irq_find_at_or_after(unsigned int offset)
>> +struct irq_desc *irq_find_desc_at_or_after(unsigned int offset)
>>  {
>>  	unsigned long index = offset;
>> -	struct irq_desc *desc;
>> -
>> -	guard(rcu)();
>
> Is the drop of guard(rcu)() intentional here? irq_get_next_irq()
> dereferences the returned pointer after mt_find() has dropped
> rcu_read_unlock(). Should it hold its own guard(rcu)() to keep the same
> behaviour as before?

Good catch. The caller of irq_find_at_or_after() needs to hold it.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/8] mm: use zone lock guard in reserve_highatomic_pageblock()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Discussion around simplifying control flow in mm functions by using zone lock guard, with trade-offs and potential overhead to consider.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about trade-offs and overhead of using zone lock guard, but acknowledged the benefits of simplifying control flow.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Expressed openness to the idea, but emphasized the need for input from those regularly working on this code and suggested a respin with updated thinking.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 26 Mar 2026 18:04:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> On Fri, Mar 06, 2026 at 09:53:36AM -0800, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
> > 
> > > Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
> > > reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
> > > goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
> > > 
> > > ...
> > >
> > > -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
> > > +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);
> > 
> > guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
> > page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
> > reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
> > footprint.
> > 
> > So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
> > time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
> > maintainability.
> > 
> > Is it worth it?
> 
> Hi Andrew,
> 
> Before respinning this series, I wanted to check if it's worth pursuing.

Probably.  Much depends on the views of the people who regularly work
on this code.  Do they like guard(), or do they prefer the current
explicit open-coded locking?

> At the time you noted the text size increase and questioned whether the
> trade-off makes sense. Since then, the guard infrastructure was fixed by
> Peter, so the code generation situation has improved.

Great.

> The main benefit of the series is still simplifying control flow in
> these functions (removing multiple unlock paths, gotos, etc.).
> 
> Would you be open to this direction if the overhead is negligible, or
> would you prefer to avoid this kind of transformation regardless?
> 
> I can also limit the series to only the more complex cases if that
> helps.

Gee.  I think it would be helpful to prepare a respin which reflects
your current thinking, see what others think.

Please understand that I'm resisting adding new material during this
cycle
(https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20260323202941.08ddf2b0411501cae801ab4c@linux-foundation.org)
so you'd best be targeting 7.1-rc1 at the earliest.

But sending out a new version during this cycle for people to consider
would be a good step.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference any symbols from linux/sched.h.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [patch v2 08/14] genirq: Add rcuref count to struct irq_desc
